coredata.dc:title: Construction of a new dual-drug delivery system based on stimuli-responsive co-polymer functionalized D-mannose for chemotherapy of breast cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Namazi, Hassan
Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Salehi, Roya
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we aimed to prepare the new blue-emissive carbon dots (CDs)-supported glyco-copolymer brushes functionalized D-mannose for targeted co-anticancer drug delivery to the breast cancer cells. Typically, the N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM) and maleic anhydride (MAH) were copolymerized on the silica functionalized CDs surface via free-radical polymerization (CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)). Then, the synthesized CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) was functionalized with D-mannose (CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man). The success of the nanocomposite synthesis was approved via several common characterization techniques. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) analysis determined the percentage of polymer brush summed with D-mannose (D-Man) at about 12%. The size of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was obtained at 30–42 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) analysis obtained an emission peak at ∼442 nm under the excitation wavelength of 365 for the nanocomposite, representing its blue luminescence nature. Doxorubicin (DOX) and curcumin (CUR) were co-loaded respectively, about ∼62% and ∼38% on CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man (DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man). The prepared system exhibited controlled drug release behavior and good biocompatibility in the in vitro cellular tests. The percentage of the arrested cells in sub G1 phase for the treated cells with DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was 100 %, while it was 86.85% for free DOX@CUR which can approve the high efficiency of DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man in cancer therapy. Overall, the results displayed that the prepared nanocomposite can be potentially proposed as an efficient carrier for controlled and targeted anticancer drug delivery along with bioimaging.

In both tests, the control is the untreated cells.

head.ce:title: Construction of a new dual-drug delivery system based on stimuli-responsive co-polymer functionalized D-mannose for chemotherapy of breast cancer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we aimed to prepare the new blue-emissive carbon dots (CDs)-supported glyco-copolymer brushes functionalized D-mannose for targeted co-anticancer drug delivery to the breast cancer cells. Typically, the N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM) and maleic anhydride (MAH) were copolymerized on the silica functionalized CDs surface via free-radical polymerization (CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)). Then, the synthesized CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) was functionalized with D-mannose (CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man). The success of the nanocomposite synthesis was approved via several common characterization techniques. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) analysis determined the percentage of polymer brush summed with D-mannose (D-Man) at about 12%. The size of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was obtained at 30–42 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) analysis obtained an emission peak at ∼442 nm under the excitation wavelength of 365 for the nanocomposite, representing its blue luminescence nature. Doxorubicin (DOX) and curcumin (CUR) were co-loaded respectively, about ∼62% and ∼38% on CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man (DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man). The prepared system exhibited controlled drug release behavior and good biocompatibility in the in vitro cellular tests. The percentage of the arrested cells in sub G1 phase for the treated cells with DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was 100 %, while it was 86.85% for free DOX@CUR which can approve the high efficiency of DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man in cancer therapy. Overall, the results displayed that the prepared nanocomposite can be potentially proposed as an efficient carrier for controlled and targeted anticancer drug delivery along with bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the pivotal oldest diseases, which has shown enhanced growth with the development of modern life. Thus, in recent years extensive efforts have been done to treat this leading death in the world [1–3]. Several ways have been developed for cancer treatment, and, among them, chemotherapy is the most commonly used treatment way [4–9]. CUR is a model of the chemotherapy drugs which is extracted from the plant roots of Curcuma longa or turmeric [10–12]. CUR commonly is used in combination with other anticancer drugs to achieve more efficiency [13–18]. DOX is a broad-spectrum efficacious antitumor chemotherapeutic drug that could be used in the combination with CUR to enhance the efficiency of the treatment. Despite the advantages of chemotherapy, the direct administration of chemotherapeutic agents leads to serious side effects in normal tissues [19–21]. Therefore, finding an appropriate anticancer drug carrier is essential [22,23]. Up to now, different types of systems like nanoparticles have been introduced for anticancer drug delivery which of them has some unique special advantages [24–27]. In between, nowadays, CDs with a particle size smaller than 10 nm made a major improvement in cancer therapy [28–31]. Good biocompatibility, excellent stability, π-π conjugated network, aromatic conjugate structure, and excellent water dispersibility are some versatile properties of CDs for use in drug delivery areas [30,32–35]. Another advantage of CDs is the possibility of their functionalization with other materials like smart polymers [36–40]. Poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) with a lower critical solution temperature (LCST) of 32 °C is the commonly used temperature-responsive polymer in anticancer drug delivery applications [41]. This thermal-sensitive phenomenon is appropriate for smart drug delivery in cancer therapy [13,42]. The copolymerization of NIPAM with other monomers may be a suitable method for further improving the system's functionality. In this area, the maleic anhydride (MAH) is a widely copolymerized monomer which expected to be effective in upgrading the efficiency of nanocarriers by providing the possibility of next functionalization with desired molecules like carbohydrates [43,44]. Additionally, targeted anticancer drug delivery could be achieved after the functionalization of the system with some carbohydrates; for example D-Man [45,46]. Numerous human malignant tumors overexpress the mannose receptor (MR) on its surface. In these types of systems, receptor-mediated endocytosis could pointedly enhance the cell internalization rate [47–49]. It is described that the MDA-MB-231 is an example of the cancer cell lines that overexpress MR on them [50]. Up to now, several systems have been evaluated as anticancer-targeted drug carriers. For example, Hafezi et al. prepared the Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs nanosystem for controlled drug delivery with ultrasound irradiation and pH-triggered [51]. In another interesting research work, Yadavalli et al. reported the suitability of the dual responsive PNIPAM-chitosan nanopolymers for targeted delivery of CUR to breast cancer cell lines [52].

From the published literature it is envisaged that up to this day, there is no study on the synthesis and evaluation of blue-emissive CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite for targeted breast cancer treatment. Therefore, considering the above-mentioned introduction and in following our study on anticancer drug delivery, in the current research work, we developed a new drug carrier taking interesting advantages of CDs, stimuli-responsive polymers, and also D-Man in enhancing the drug loading percentage, targeted drug delivery, and improving the biocompatibility. The formation of the nanocomposite was verified via common characterization techniques. Then the CUR and DOX co-delivery potential of the nanocomposite was studied. Also, the in vitro biocompatibility and inherent bioimaging potential were studied. The achieved results can propose that the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man has some brilliant potential as a theranostic agent for Man-overexpressing breast cancer treatment.

Ascorbic acid (As, ≥99.0%), toluene (≥99.5%), anhydrous ethanol, dioxane (≥99.0%), D-Man (≥99.0%), methanol (≥99.0%), ethylenediamine (≥99.0%), triethylamine (TEA, ≥99.5%), phenol (≥99.0%), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF ≥ 99.9%), and sulfuric acid (95.0–98.0%) were purchased from Merck. Triethoxyvinylsilane (TEVS, 97%), NIPAM (97%), MAH (99%), 2,2′-Azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN, 98%), 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), iodine (≥99.99%), 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH, 97%), Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 Medium, CUR (≥94%), propidium iodide (PI, ≥94%), and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, ≥95.0%) were products of Sigma-Aldrich. Fetal bovine serum albumin (FBS), ribonuclease A (RnaseA), trypsin, penicillin–streptomycin, and the other used biological materials provided by Gibco. DOX was supplied by Sobhan Pharmaceuticals Co. (Iran). Double distilled water was employed overall the work. The selected MDA-MB-231 and HUVEC cell lines for doing the cellular studies were attained from Pasteur Institute (Iranian National Cell Bank, Tehran, Iran). The used mannosylated ethylenediamine was prepared according to the previously published paper [53].

The optical property of the samples was recorded by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 1700 UV–Vis spectrophotometer) and Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan). For the PL test, samples were irradiated under a UV light of 365 nm. The surface properties of the samples were determined with a TESCANMIRA3 scanning electron microscope (SEM). Also, the X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) spectra and elemental mapping were acquired with this equipment. Nanoscale sizes were calculated with ImageJ software. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Bruker Instruments, model Aquinox 55, Germany) was used to study the functional groups of the materials. The mean hydrodynamic diameters were measured using a DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave). TGA measurements were carried out by using the Linseise STA PT-1000 instrument under the N2 atmosphere. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were gained using the LEO 906E transmission electron microscope. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis was carried out employing a DSC instrument (Linseis-L63/45, Germany) at the heating rate of 10 °C/min. PHS-3C pH meter (A digital pH meter) was used for pH determination.

CDs were fabricated via the hydrothermal method using the AS as the raw material [29]. Typically, As (3 g) was dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water to achieve a clear solution. Then the solution was moved to a Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave and then heated for 24 h at 200 °C. After that, the obtained orange-brown luminescent mixture was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged to eliminate the black precipitates. The attained supernatant was filtered, water was removed from the supernatant under reduced pressure, and the produced CDs were dried in the vacuum oven. Eventually, the achieved solid was kept in a dark vial for further usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of CDs with vinylic functional groups (CDs-VTES)
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were functionalized with TEVS to link the vinyl functional groups on its surface. Briefly, about 0.75 g of CDs were dispersed in anhydrous toluene (40 mL) for 30 min. Then the TEVS solution (2 mL of TEVS in 15 mL of dry toluene) was added dropwise to the previous mixture. The mixture was refluxed for 24 h. The resultant was centrifuged, washed several times with anhydrous toluene and ethanol, and dried in a vacuum oven [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) on the CDs-VTES surface (CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH))
ce:section.ce:para: For the growth of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) on the CDs-VTES surface, at first, the TEVS-modified CDs (0.50 g) were dispersed into dry dioxane (80 mL). Then, under the argon gas flow, NIPAM (4.6 g) and MAH (0.46 g) were poured into the reaction mixture while stirring. AIBN (0.342 g) was slowly added to the reaction medium and then the mixture was heated at 70 °C for 12 h. After finishing the reaction, the produced CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) was purified by centrifuging through repeated washing with dry dioxane and ethanol. Lastly, the purified sample was dried in a vacuum oven.

Synthesis of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was accomplished by amidation of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) (80 mg) with mannosylated ethylenediamine (120 mg) into a flask containing DMF (20 mL) as a solvent and TEA as the catalyst. The mixture was stirred at 50 °C overnight. Then, the resulting CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was centrifuged, washed, and dried in a vacuum oven [55]. The overall flowchart of the synthesis procedure is presented in section 3.1, Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative evaluation of the D-Man presence in CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure [55] for approving the presence of D-Man in the structure of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man, at first, an aqueous dispersion of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man (10 mg/mL) was prepared. In the following, to the reaction vessel, about 50 μL of phenol aqueous solution (80%) was added followed by the addition of 5 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid. The mixture was incubated for 10 min at ambient temperature, shaken, and then located for 20 min in the water bath at room temperature. Finally, the UV–Vis spectrum of the solution was recorded by employing a UV–Vis spectrophotometer to use the appeared intensity of the maximum peak for elucidating the D-Man percentage in the sample by considering the initial weight of the sample and the standard curve of the D-Man.

DPPH radical scavenging test was accomplished to evaluate the antioxidant activity of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man and DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man. Briefly, at first, the suspension of the sample (1 mg) was prepared in 2 mL of methanol. Next, the methanolic DPPH solution (2 mL, 40 μg/mL) was added to the test tube. The mixtures were shaken for 30 min in a dark medium. Then the absorbance of the supernatant was determined by employing a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 517 nm. Subsequently, the free radical scavenging capacity was measured (%) using the following equation:
(1)

Where the A1 and A0 respectively are the absorbance of the sample and standard solution of DPPH in equal conditions.

The in vitro colloidal stability of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite was studied to visually investigate its behavior in different media from the stability and aggregation viewpoints. Generally, sample with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL separately was dispensed in four different environments; water, PBS (pH 7.4), FBS, and RPMI containing 10% (V/V) of FBS. Then each of the suspensions slightly was shaken for 72 h at 37 °C. In the end, the stability of each dispersion was evaluated through visual monitoring.

The dual drug delivery potential of the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite was investigated for loading of both CUR and DOX. In this regard, at first, 30 mg of each sample was dispersed in an ethanolic solution of CUR (5 mg). Then, the mixture was shaken at room temperature and in a dark place. After 24 h, the CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was separated from the drug solution and washed to eliminate the unloaded CUR. The absorbance of the supernatant was obtained via UV–Vis spectroscopy and utilized to calculate the drug loading value. Afterward, the CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was dispersed in DOX solution (5 mg) and the mixture was shaken in a dark place at ambient temperature. Next, the dual drug-loaded samples were separated from the DOX solution, washed several times with PBS, vacuum dried, and then the absorbance of the supernatant was obtained by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Eventually, the drug loading capacity and drug entrapment efficiency were calculated for both drugs obtained according to the following equations:

Because the percentage of the drug loading and cell viability for the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was more than CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH), we performed the drug release and other biological tests only for CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man as the optimum system. At first, the temperature-sensitive drug release capability of DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was tested at a constant pH of 7.4 but at two temperatures of 37 °C and 40 °C. Then the anticancer drug release study was performed in two different environments containing SDS; (I) the simulated pH and temperature of the physiological condition (pH 7.4, 37 °C) and (II) the simulated pH and temperature of the tumor medium (pH 5.0, 40 °C). Typically, a dispersion of DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man (3 mL of release medium) was prepared, and then it was moved into a dialysis bag. The mixture was dialyzed toward 7 mL of release medium while continuously shaking at a dark place. At specific times, about 3 mL of the released drugs were sampled and the same volume of fresh buffer was replaced with it. The amount of released drug (%) was calculated by monitoring the supernatant absorbance, standard calibration curve, and Eq. 5.

The drug load and release tests were accomplished three times and the outcome is the average of these three replicates.

A series of in vitro cellular tests were performed to evaluate the biocompatibility, bioimaging potential, as well as targeted drug delivery potential of the synthesized system. In all of these tests, the cells with no treatment were considered as control. Additionally, these tests were performed three times and the data are reported as average.

The relative cytotoxicity of the samples was investigated toward MDA-MB 231 (a model of the cancer cell overexpressed MR) and HUVEC (a model of the normal cell) cells via the MTT test. In this way, the selected cells were seeded in the 96-well plates at a concentration of 104 cells/well. After overnight incubation in an atmosphere of 5% carbon dioxide (CO2) and 95% humidity, the seeded cells in the 96-well plates were treated with the samples and then incubated for 48 h at 37 °C. In the following, the unused culture medium was separated and 200 μL of the MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was poured into each well and the cells were incubated at 37 °C. After 4 h, the unreacted MTT solution was drained and 200 μL of DMSO was added to each well. The plates were shaken for 15 min on the shaker incubator to dissolve the created formazan crystals. The ELISA plate reader was employed to obtain the optical density of each well at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm.

Both qualitative (fluorescent microscopy) and quantitative (flow cytometry) tests were employed to examine and compare the capability of the DOX@CUR and DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man for MDA-MB 231 cellular internalization. For qualitative procedures, the MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded over the sterile coverslip which was deposited in each well of the 6-well plates. After 24 h incubation, the unused medium was discarded and the seeded cells were treated with the samples for 3 h. The cells were rinsed with PBS and the coverslips were immobilized onto the glass microscope slides to observe the qualitative cell uptake by means of Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA as a fluorescence microscope. In the following, to further confirm the results of qualitative cell uptake, the MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded in 6-well plates and incubated overnight at 37 °C. The attached cells were treated with the samples and incubation was continued for 3 h. After that, the cells were trypsinized, harvested, rinsed, and finally examined via the FACScalibur flow cytometer (Becton Dickinson Immunocytometry Systems, San Jose, CA, USA). The reported flow cytometry data for the quantitative cell uptake test are the average of three replicates.

For the in vitro bioimaging study, the MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded on the coverslip located on each well of 6-well plates. Afterward, the cells were incubated at 37 °C overnight. The seeded cells were treated with CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite at two different times; 0.5 h and 3 h. After each incubation time, the culture medium was discharged, cells rinsed with PBS, and then imaged employing a fluorescence microscope (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the nuclear morphology via DAPI staining
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the treatment with DOX@CUR, CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man, and DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man on the nucleus morphology was investigated via the DAPI staining technique. Generally, the selected MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded on the sterile coverslip deposited in each well of the 6-well plates and then incubated at 37 °C overnight. The attached MDA-MB 231 cells were treated with the samples and incubated for another 48 h. The cells were rinsed with PBS and stabled with paraformaldehyde (3 wt%) at room temperature. After 10 min, the extra paraformaldehyde was eliminated via rinsing of the fixed cells with PBS. The cells were permeabilized with Triton X-100 (0.1% w/v), washed with PBS, and finally stained with DAPI dye for 5 min. Eventually, the safety or apoptotic nuclei (fragmented or wrinkled) were pictured employing a fluorescence microscope (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cycle evaluation via flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: The cycle pattern of MDA-MB 231 cell after treatment with DOX@CUR, CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH), and DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was explored to study the effect of treatment with each of them. In a typical procedure, the cells were seeded in 6-well plates and then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The attached cells were treated with the samples and the incubation was followed for 48 h at 37 °C. After that, the cells were washed, trypsinized, gathered, stabled with cold ethanol (70%), and then stored at 4 °C. After 72 h, the cells respectively stained with the PI solution and RnaseA (Cinagen). Finally, the cell population in each phase of the cell cycle was specified by using the FACSCalibur (Becton Dickinson Immunocytometry Systems, San Jose, CA, USA). The reported outcome is the average of three replicates of the culturing.

Due to the increased carbohydrate levels in cancer cells, recently D-Man linked nanomaterials have been introduced as beneficial systems for targeted cancer therapy. Moreover, obtaining a new drug carrier having both photoluminescent and antioxidant features is valuable in cancer therapy. By considering these, in the current work, for the first time, a new CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite was synthesized for targeted co-drug delivery. Then several characterization techniques were used to analyze the structure of the developed nanosystem. Then the capability of the prepared system was investigated for the controlled and targeted co-delivery of DOX and CUR. Eventually, in vitro biological tests were accomplished to evaluate its biocompatibility, bioimaging capability, and cancer cell-killing ability after drug loading. The overall flowchart of the synthesis procedure and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man capability for co-drug load and release are shown in Fig. 1.

FT-IR spectroscopy was carried out to monitor the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man synthesis process and also the co-loading of CUR and DOX on it (Fig. 2 ). In the FT-IR spectrum of AS, the bands at 3150–3400 cm−1, 1709 cm−1, and 1594 cm−1 respectively are related to stretching vibration of the hydroxyl groups, C = O of the five-membered lactone ring, and stretching vibration of C = C. The as-prepared CDs provided the stretching vibration mode of the O–H, C–H, C-O, and also the C–H out-of-plane bending modes respectively at 3000–3500 cm−1, 2932 cm−1, 1076 cm−1, 855 cm−1 of its FT-IR spectrum. In addition, the appeared bands at 1588 cm−1 and 1414 cm−1 are owing to stretching vibrations of the C = O and C = C. In comparison with the FT-IR spectrum of the AS, the appearance of the intense C = C peak for CDs could be a reason for the successful synthesis of CDs. Moreover, the appearance of the related bands to the oxygen-containing functional groups can approve the possibility of the CDs silanization with TEVS. The modified CDs with TEVS emerged two new absorption bands at 2905 cm−1 and 2926 cm−1 of its FT-IR spectrum that are related to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of vinylic –CH and their appearance can specify the successful functionalization of the CDs with TEVS. After doing the free radical polymerization, the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of C–H in isopropyl groups was observed at 2923 cm−1 and 2857 cm−1. In this spectrum the detected peaks at 1698 cm−1, 1559 cm−1, and 1457 cm−1 respectively correspond to the vibration of the C = O (amide), –NH, and –CH(CH3)2 groups. Additionally, the observed bands at 1814 cm−1 and 1703 cm−1 correspond to the anhydride units in MAH. The appearance of the mentioned peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) displays the successful copolymerization of the NIPAM and MAH on the CDs-TEVS. After mannosylation, the band at 1703 cm−1 disappeared and a new peak appeared at 1659 cm−1 which can be related to the ring opening of the MAH. Additionally, the characteristic peak of D-Man was noticed at 1070 cm−1 due to the C-O stretching of D-Man. The summation of the FT-IR analysis findings specifies the successful synthesis of the final nanocomposite.

In the continue, the FT-IR analysis was utilized to explore the success in the co-drug loading of DOX and CUR on CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite. As presented in Fig. 2b, the FT-IR spectrum of the DOX displayed the peaks at 889 cm−1, 1117 cm−1, 1724 cm−1, 2920 cm−1, and 3422 cm−1 which are consistent with the stretching vibration of the primary amine, C-O-C, C = O, C–H, –OH, and –NH groups. CUR showed the bands at 3506 cm−1, 1626 cm−1, 1508 cm−1, 1426 cm−1, and 1276 cm−1 of its FT-IR spectrum, respectively indicating the presence of phenolic –OH, C = O of enol group, C = C of the aromatic ring, C–H bending vibration of alkane, and C-O stretching vibrations of alcohol. Also, the observed peaks at 857 cm−1 and 812 cm−1 were attributed to out-of-plane bending vibration of meta- and para-substituted aromatic systems. In the case of DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man, a mathematic combination of these peaks with partial shifts occurred which can be evidence for the CUR and DOX co-loading on nanocomposite. Overall, the occurring possible interactions between both drugs and nanocarrier achieved the DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man in success.

The optical property of the synthesized CDs, as well as CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man, was explored by employing UV–Vis spectroscopy to obtain and compare the optical absorption properties of the CDs and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man. The obtained spectra are shown in Fig. 3 a. The CDs displayed two absorption peaks in the studied wavelength range. One of these peaks was detected at around ∼ 280 nm indicating π-π* transition of C = C bonds in sp2 hybrid regions. The second was as a shoulder at ∼ 336 nm, signifying the n-π* transition of the C = O bond. The nanocomposite showed similar absorption peaks with reduced intensity and a slight shift that may be due to the functionalization effects [29]. In the following, this analysis also was accomplished to certify the presence of D-Man in the nanocomposite structure (Fig. 3b). Generally, the phenol–sulfuric acid was utilized for the qualitative approval of the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) functionalization with D-Man, as well as for the determination of its quantitative percentage in the sample. In this test, the appearance of the absorbance peak at 490 nm exhibited the successful functionalization of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) with D-Man. Additionally, considering the detected adsorption peak intensity, the standard calibration curve of D-Man, and the weight of the used sample, the D-Man percentage was determined about 16.04%.

To assess the PL property, the aqueous dispersion of CDs and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite was transferred to a quartz cuvette, and then the PL spectra were obtained under the same excitation wavelength; λex 365. For CDs, the PL spectrum exhibited one emission peak at ∼ 442 nm, representing its blue luminescence nature. Moreover, the nanocomposite dispersion showed an emission peak at a similar wavelength under the same excitation irradiation. In comparison with CDs, the nanocomposite exhibited a lower PL intensity. The formed new chemistry owing to the functionalization of CDs, which is able to affect the relaxation/electron transfer process probably is the reason for this difference. Inconclusive the detected PL property (Fig. 3d) for CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man is a sign of its bio-imaging potential [56,57]. For further approval of the fluorescent properties of the final system, the photographs of the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man dispersion under visible light and also UV light were obtained and reported in Fig. 3d.

EDX spectra of the CDs and CDs-TEVS were recorded to investigate and approve the successful conjugation of CDs with TEVS through a survey of their chemical elemental composition. As it is shown in Fig. S1a, the synthesized CDs showed only the peaks related to the C and O elements. While the EDX spectrum of the CDs-TEVS demonstrated the peaks related to the C, O, and Si elements. The appearance of the Si peak can approve the successful modification of the CDs with TEVS. In the following, the elemental map of the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was obtained to explore how the formation of the copolymer from the homogeneity viewpoint. As illustrated in Fig. 1Sc, the Si and N elements are uniformly distributed on the nanocomposite structure. This not only could confirm the copolymer's growth but also can approve its good distribution. Through the above data, it can be concluded that the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was successfully prepared.

The thermal stability of the CDs, CDs-TEVS, and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite was analyzed under an N2 atmosphere from room temperature to 600 °C, estimating the grafting polymer density at elevated temperatures. The achieved plots are exhibited in Fig. 4 a. In the studied heating process the bare CDs showed a char yield of about 60.5 % corresponding to the loss of absorbed water and decomposition of some of the organic functional groups. On the other hand, CDs-TEVS exhibited a 36.7 % of mass loss, which was lower than neat CDs. These results proposed that the functionalization of GQDs with TEVS might assist in enhancing thermal stability. Compared with CDs-TEVS, the thermogravimetric curve of nanocomposite indicated a much mass loss and the maximum mass loss ratio was up to 48.7%, which can be attributed to the grafted copolymer and D-Man on the CDs surface. Eventually, the percentage of the grown polymer summed with D-Man is estimated at 12%. Ultimately, the TGA results supported the FT-IR and EDX results which displayed the silanization of CDs, and also the introduction of polymer chains and D-Man in the structure of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was performed correctly.

The LSCT measurement of each newly prepared system is an effective method that can be used to evaluate its temperature sensitivity. Therefore, the DSC test was conducted to determine the LCST of the fabricated CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 4b the thermogram showed a noticeable endothermic peak with the highest intensity approximately at the temperature of 35.5 °C. Indeed, at this temperature, the fabricated system undergoes a coil-to-globule transition which is a beneficial feature of each anticancer drug carrier from the controlled drug delivery viewpoint. In comparison with neat PNIPAM [58], the observed shift in the transition temperature of nanocomposite to the higher values might be due to the introduction of hydrophilic compounds and their hybridization and is consistent with previously reported results. This result indicates that the fabricated nanosystem could be used as a smart drug delivery vehicle in cancer therapy.

After detailed confirmation of the successful synthesis of the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite by FT-IR, DLS analysis was used to find the hydrodynamic diameter changes of CDs as starting material and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite preparation as the final product. The corresponding results are listed in Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d. As is shown, both CDs and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man presented narrow size distribution respectively with a mean hydrodynamic size of CDs and nanocomposite 5.23 nm and 200.7 nm. The observed higher hydrodynamic diameter for CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man than CDs can be related to the successful silanization, copolymer growth, and finally functionalization with D-Man moieties.

Size and morphology are the main criteria of each drug delivery system. Considering this, the SEM and TEM analyses were performed on CDs and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man to explore the how change in these parameres as a result of the performed modification reactions on CDs. SEM is a powerful technique to perform analysis on the morphology and size of a wide range of materials. Fig. 5 a demonstrates the surface morphology of the prepared CDs. As observed, the synthesized CDs exhibited a homogeneous spherical appearance with an average diameter of about 4 nm [54,59]. When the CDs were functionalized, the fabricated CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite showed a similar morphology to CDs but its diameter was distinctive. It can be seen from the particle size distribution graph (insertion in Fig. 4b) that the particle size range of nanocomposite was between 30 and 42 nm. The observed size increase suggests that the silanization, copolymerization, and modification with D-Man were performed well and the detected trend is similar to the DLS analysis. In the following, to have a better understanding of CDs and nanocomposite morphology their analysis was carried out using the TEM technique. According to the TEM results, uniform spherical morphology was observed for both CDs and nanocomposite, which is consistent with the SEM outcome. In the case of both SEM and TEM analysis, the observed partial aggregations can be related to the interaction between the chains and are in good agreement with the published works [54,59].

The radical scavenging activity of the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man and DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was studied via the colorimetric method to assess their antioxidant potential. Generally, the DPPH radicals with a maximum absorption peak at 519 nm were selected for this test. The achieved UV–Vis spectra for the control (free DPPH solution) and samples after 30 min incubation with DPPH are shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b. As it is presented both CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man and DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man showed very lower absorbance than the control at maximum absorbance which exhibits their significantly higher radical scavenging activity, respectively with a value of ∼ 66.15% and 75.89%. The antioxidant activity of the nanocomposites arises from the CDs with As origin and is a valuable character for each anticancer drug carrier. Additionally, the relatively higher antioxidant activity of DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man than CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man can be attributed to loaded CUR which has the antioxidant nature.

Virtual monitoring was used to detect the colloidal stability of the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite in four different mediums over 3 days. Typically, the taken digital photos of the freshly prepared dispersion, and also the dispersion after 3 days are shown in Fig. 6c. From these images, it was found that there is no macroscopic change like precipitation or aggregation which means that our prepared system is stable over the studied experimental time. This observation confirmed that the prepared system is stable in the physiological solutions and could be used for biomedicinal applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro co-drug load and release study
ce:section.ce:para: To explore and compare the capability of the designed CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man as the controlled drug delivery systems, CUR and DOX were sequentially loaded on it. Afterward, based on the supernatant absorbance the drug loading percentages were estimated (Fig. 6d). As reported the functionalization with D-Man increased the drug loading percentage which may be related to the presence of more polar functional groups on its structure, as well as, the appearance of the carboxylic acid groups on the structure of the carrier as a result of the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) reaction with mannosylated ethylene diamine could be another reason. H-bonding, electrostatic, and π–π stacking interactions are the possible interactions between drug molecules and designed nanocarriers. For more evaluation, the drug loading findings of the current research work were compared with the published studies for CUR and DOX loading. As reported in Table 1 , the prepared system has acceptable drug loading percentages in comparison with the others, displaying its efficiency as a promising drug delivery system for anticancer drug loading.

In the following of the current work, the temperature-sensitive nature of the DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was explored. For this, the release of both CUR and DOX was examined at a constant pH of 7.4 but at two different temperatures. The obtained polts are shown in Fig. 7 a and Fig. 7b. As presented the system has temperature sensitive nature and most of both drugs were released at a higher temperature, approving CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man suitability in cancer therapy due to the higher temperature of cancer tissue than the normal cells. Afterward, the DOX@CUR release behavior was assessed at two different conditions, simulated with normal and cancer cell conditions. The achieved results are presented in Fig. 7c and Fig. 7d. As can be seen, both of the loaded drugs were released at an initial fast rate which was followed by a sustained and prolonged release. The observed profile could be attributed respectively to attached drug molecules on the outer surface of the nanocarrier and the incorporated drug molecules within the nanocarrier structure. From this Fig, it was found that the releases of both drugs were noticeably suppressed at (pH 7.4, 37 °C), so the cumulative release percentage over 72 h only was 26.7% (for DOX) and 17.4% (for CUR). Comparatively, about 41.5% of CUR and 68% of DOX were released from DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man under the condition of (pH 5.0 °C, 40 °C). These findings specified that the DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man has pH and temperature sensitivity with more stability in the simulated physiological conditions [6,64]. The pH and temperature sensitivity of the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man respectively may result because of the presence of carboxylic acid and hydroxyl functional groups and PNIPAM portions in the carrier structure. Formation of the electrostatic repulsion between the deprotonated oxygen-containing functional groups of the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man and drug molecules as well as breaking of the π-π stacking interaction could be reasonable explanations for the quick drug releases in the acidic conditions. Besides, a breakage of the π-π stacking interactions and expansion of the polymer brushes at the higher temperature are the leading factors in accelerating the drug releases. Overall it can be concluded that the designed CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man with a much lower drug release in the simulated physiological medium and a higher percentage of drug release in tumor-simulated conditions demonstrate the desirability for controlled anticancer drug delivery. So, such features would effectively decrease the side and toxic effects of used chemotherapeutic drugs on normal cells and obtain cancer therapeutic with higher efficiency.

The cytotoxicity of the samples was tested against MDA-MB 231 and HUVEC cell lines using an MTT assay (Fig. 8 ). For the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man samples both of the studied cell lines were viable higher than 80% after 48 h incubation, demonstrating that the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) and CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man are a biocompatible drug carrier. Additionally, it was detected that the functionalization with D-Man increased the biocompatibility of the final system. The viabilities of the treated cells with DOX@CUR and DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man decreased as drug concentrations increased. The final MDA-MB 231 cells viabilities for the treated cells with free DOX@CUR and DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man were 20.4% and 14.1%, respectively. Additionally, the DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man presented higher cytotoxicity than DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) on MDA-MB 231 that shows the positive influence of functionalization with D-Man in receiving the targeted drug delivery, cell viability of DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) was determined at about 18%. Meanwhile, both nanosystems presented significantly higher cytotoxicity compared to the free DOX@CUR group across all drug concentrations. This observation may be due to the enhanced cellular uptake when the drug is carried with the nanocarrier. Besides, the cell viability of HUVEC was measured and the result indicated higher cell viability than MDA-MB 231. The lower cytotoxicity for HUVEC than MDA-MB 231 can further verify the versatile targeted drug delivery potential of the fabricated CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man for breast cancer therapy, results from the D-Man introduction in the structure [65,66]. So, the survey of the published literature shows that the results are in good accordance with published articles in reliable sources [67–69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake, qualitative and quantitative analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The qualitative cellular uptakes of DOX@CUR and DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man in MDA-MB 231 cells were observed employing a fluorescence microscope. In this way, the cells were treated with the studied samples and afterward incubated for 3 h. The obtained images are presented in Fig. 9 a and Fig. 9b. As can be seen, the treated cells with DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man exhibited red and green fluorescence, indicating the release of DOX and CUR from DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man and their internalization within the cells by endocytosis or diffusion to the cytoplasm as a result of DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man cellular uptake. In comparison with DOX@CUR, the fluorescence intensity for DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was higher, which might be due to the efficient uptaking of the drug molecules after loading on the nanostructures.

To further verify the qualitative results, the content of DOX in cells was analyzed employing flow cytometry quantitatively. As presented in Fig. 9c, Fig. 9d, and Fig. 9e the amount of the internalized DOX increased in the following order: free DOX@CUR < DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man, which was consistent with the results of fluorescence microscopic images and display the enhanced cellular uptaking for DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man due to its special structure.

The potential of CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite for bioimaging application was explored based on its inherent excellent fluorescence properties. As depicted in Fig. 9f and Fig. 9g the fluorescence image of the cells was taken after MDA-MB 231 cells treatment with nanocomposite for 0.5 h and 3 h. As presented the cell internalization was enhanced by increasing the contact time from 0.5 h to 3 h. The detected blue fluorescent intensity for the treated cells confirms the potential of the system for bioimaging applications.

DAPI staining test (Fig. 10 a) was accomplished to illustrate the fragmentation and condensing of the dead cell's DNA. As it is reported, the treatment of the MDA-MB 231 cells with drug-free nanocomposite did not lead to any significant toxic effects, so, the fragmentations and condensing of DNA were not distinguished. However, the treated cells with both DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man and free DOX@CUR displayed chromatin fragmentation. In addition, the rate of chromatin fragmentation for the treated cells with DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was more than free DOX@CUR, suggesting that the fabricated CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man is an excellent drug delivery system with biocompatible nature and enhanced efficiency for cancer treatment after drug loading.

Cell cycle analysis was performed employing flow cytometry to prove DNA duplication in cell cycle phases. As presented in Fig. 10b, in comparison with non-treated cells (control) the treated cells with CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocomposite did not display a notable difference in a cell cycle pattern. This finding verified that the fabricated CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man is biocompatible. However, the treated cells with DOX@CUR and DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man showed an arrest in the sub G1 phase. The content of the arrested cells in sub G1 phase for the treated cells with DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man was higher than free DOX@CUR (free DOX@CUR: 86.85%, DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man: 100%) which exhibit that the DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man is very effective than its free DOX@CUR. A more percentage of the sub G1 population can verify apoptotic in the cell cycle and cell death as a result of it. These results are in the agreement with MTT and DAPI outcomes and confirm the suitability of the nanocomposite for use as a drug carrier.

In summary, the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH) was synthesized via copolymerization of MAH and NIPAM on modified CDs, which were then functionalized with D-Man. In fact a novel macromolecule with all of the targeted co-drug delivery, pH, and temperature sensitivity was designed and prepared to obtain improved cancer cells' treatment efficiency. The obtained SEM showed the mean diameter of the fabricated system as about 30–42 nm. TGA analysis determined the percentage of the grafted poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man about 12%. The resulting nanocomposite was co-loaded with DOX and CUR. The DOX@CUR@CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man demonstrated the thermo-responsive and pH-sensitive nature. In the MTT test, the fabricated CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man exhibited no obvious side effect on MDA-MB 231 cell viability, even at a high dosage of 300 µg/mL. However, a notable inhibition ratio was observed when these cells were treated with the DOX@CUR-loaded CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man nanocarrier. Additionally, the results of the DAPI and cell cycle test were in good accordance with MTT, suggesting that the biocompatible CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man is able to enhance the therapeutic efficacy of drugs. In comparison with other anticancer drug carriers, CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man showed combined targeted drug delivery, antioxidant feature, and bioimaging properties as the main advantages for each newly designed biomedicine agent. In conclusion, the obtained results display that the CDs-poly(NIPAM-co-MAH)-D-Man could be presented as a targeted and non-toxic system for controlled human breast cancer treatment and can be used in the clinic after detailed in vivo studies in the future.
